title: Charmed Life - Chapter 5 (Fragment 4)
text:
Cat was so busy thinking of all this that, though he noticed a four-wheel cab standing in the road as he turned the corner by the Willing Warlock’s house, he paid no attention to it. He went to the side door and burst into the kitchen with his hatful of apples, shouting, “I say! Look what I’ve got, Mrs. Sharp!”

Mrs. Sharp was not there. Instead, standing in the middle of the kitchen, was a tall and quite extraordinarily well-dressed man.

Cat stared at him in some dismay. He was clearly a rich new Town Councillor. Nobody but those kind of people wore trousers with such pearly stripes, or coats of such beautiful velvet, or carried tall hats as shiny as their boots. The man’s hair was dark. It was smooth as his hat. Cat had no doubt that this was Gwendolen’s Dark Stranger, come to help her start ruling the world. And he should not have been in the kitchen at all. Visitors were always taken straight to the parlor.

“Oh, how do you do, sir. Will you come this way, sir?” he gasped.

The Dark Stranger gave him a wondering look. And well he might, Cat thought, looking around distractedly. The kitchen was in its usual mess. The range was all ash. On the table, Cat saw, to his further dismay, Mrs. Sharp had been making gingerbread men. The ingredients for the spell lay on one end of the table—all grubby newspaper packets and seedy little jars—and the gingerbread itself was strewn over the middle of the table. At the far end, the flies were gathering around the meat for lunch, which looked nearly as messy as the spell.

“Who are you?” said the Dark Stranger. “I have a feeling I should know you. What have you got in your hat?”

Cat was too busy staring around to attend properly, but he caught the last question. His pleasure returned. “Apples,” he said, showing the Stranger. “Lovely sweet ones. I’ve been scrumping.”

The Stranger looked grave. “Scrumping,” he said, “is a form of stealing.”

Cat knew that as well as he did. He thought it was very joyless, even for a Town Councillor, to point it out. “I know. But I bet you did it when you were my age.”

The Stranger coughed slightly and changed the subject. “You haven’t said yet who you are.”

“Sorry. Didn’t I?” said Cat. “I’m Eric Chant—only they always call me Cat.”

“Then is Gwendolen Chant your sister?” the Stranger asked. He was looking more and more austere and pitying. Cat suspected that he thought Mrs. Sharp’s kitchen was a den of vice.

“That’s right. Won’t you come this way?” Cat said, hoping to get the Stranger out of it. “It’s neater through here.”

“I had a letter from your sister,” the Stranger said, standing where he was. “She gave me the impression you had drowned with your parents.”

“You must have made a mistake,” Cat said distractedly. “I didn’t drown because I was holding on to Gwendolen, and she’s a witch. It’s cleaner through here.”

vocabulary.definition: Severe or strict in manner, attitude, or appearance.
vocabulary.example: He was looking more and more austere and pitying.

vocabulary.definition: To suddenly enter a place, especially with force or excitement.
vocabulary.example: He burst into the kitchen with his hatful of apples.

vocabulary.definition: Part of a piece of clothing that encircles the neck.
vocabulary.example: The Stranger wore tall hats as shiny as their boots.

vocabulary.definition: A member of a council, especially in local government.
vocabulary.example: He was clearly a rich new Town Councillor.

vocabulary.definition: A place associated with immoral or illegal activities.
vocabulary.example: Cat suspected that he thought Mrs. Sharp’s kitchen was a den of vice.

vocabulary.definition: Concern and distress caused by something unexpected.
vocabulary.example: Cat stared at him in some dismay.

vocabulary.definition: In a manner showing that one’s attention is diverted or preoccupied.
vocabulary.example: Cat said distractedly.

vocabulary.definition: To an unusual or remarkable degree.
vocabulary.example: He was clearly a rich new Town Councillor. Nobody but those kind of people wore trousers with such pearly stripes, or coats of such beautiful velvet, or carried tall hats as shiny as their boots.

vocabulary.definition: At the greatest distance.
vocabulary.example: On the table, Cat saw, to his further dismay.

vocabulary.definition: A group of people or things assembled together.
vocabulary.example: The flies were gathering around the meat for lunch.

vocabulary.definition: To seize and hold firmly; to understand fully.
vocabulary.example: He caught the last question.

vocabulary.definition: Serious or solemn in manner or appearance.
vocabulary.example: The Stranger looked grave.

translations.ru: полная шляпа (количество, которое помещается в шляпу)

vocabulary.definition: An amount enough to fill a hat.
vocabulary.example: He burst into the kitchen with his hatful of apples.

vocabulary.definition: An idea, feeling, or opinion about something or someone, especially one formed without conscious thought.
vocabulary.example: She gave me the impression you had drowned.

vocabulary.definition: Components or elements that make up a mixture or compound, especially in cooking.
vocabulary.example: The ingredients for the spell lay on one end of the table.

vocabulary.definition: Without joy; dull or bleak in character.
vocabulary.example: He thought it was very joyless, even for a Town Councillor.

vocabulary.definition: To ignore or not notice something.
vocabulary.example: He paid no attention to it.

vocabulary.definition: Feeling or showing sorrow and compassion for someone else's suffering or misfortune.
vocabulary.example: He was looking more and more austere and pitying.

vocabulary.definition: To steal fruit from someone else's trees, especially publicly or illegally.
vocabulary.example: I’ve been scrumping.

vocabulary.definition: Reflecting light; bright or polished.
vocabulary.example: Tall hats as shiny as their boots.

vocabulary.definition: A form of words supposed to have magic power.
vocabulary.example: The ingredients for the spell lay on one end of the table.

vocabulary.definition: To begin gaining control or dominance over everything or everyone.
vocabulary.example: Come to help her start ruling the world.

vocabulary.definition: To scatter or spread things untidily over a surface or area.
vocabulary.example: The gingerbread itself was strewn over the middle of the table.

vocabulary.definition: To follow a particular path or direction.
vocabulary.example: ‘Will you come this way, sir?’ he gasped.

vocabulary.definition: To change direction by turning at a corner; often means to successfully change a situation.
vocabulary.example: He turned the corner by the Willing Warlock’s house.

vocabulary.definition: A type of cloth with a soft, thick surface made of woven silk, cotton, or nylon.
vocabulary.example: Coats of such beautiful velvet.
